pattern_recognition: Greedy Algorithm - This problem can be categorized under greedy algorithms as it involves making local optimal choices (jumps) to achieve a global goal (reaching the last index). The signals include the ability to jump from various indices and the need to assess the maximum reachable index at each step.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Try all possible jumps from each index recursively to see if the last index can be reached.
approaches.algorithm: Use recursion to explore all jump possibilities, backtracking if a path does not lead to the last index.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use a greedy strategy to keep track of the furthest index that can be reached at any point and update it as you iterate through the array.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through the array while maintaining the maximum reachable index and check if it covers the last index.

dry_run: Consider the input array nums = [2, 3, 1, 1, 4]. Start at index 0, where the max jump is 2. From index 0, you can jump to index 1 or index 2. Jumping to index 1 (value 3), you can reach index 4 directly. Thus, you can reach the last index.
edge_cases: An empty input should return false, a single element array should return true, arrays with all zeros except the last element should return false, large inputs should be handled efficiently, and ensure no integer overflow occurs during calculations.
common_pitfalls: Common mistakes include: 1) Not considering that you can jump over indices, leading to premature termination of checks. 2) Failing to update the maximum reachable index correctly, causing incorrect results. 3) Overlooking edge cases like arrays with a single element or all zeros.
